name: Back Squat

common_substitutions.context: Cannot externally rotate to bar position without anterior shoulder pain
Front-loaded position reduces shear; better for disc-sensitive lifters
Teaches squat pattern with load anteriorly counterbalancing
Closed-chain knee extension without spinal load

common_cues: Drive your back into the bar
Spread the floor with your feet
Stand up and squeeze glutes hard

injury_contraindications.notes: Acute disc bulge or annular tear: substitute belt squat or leg press until cleared. Heavy axial load increases intradiscal pressure.
Patellofemoral pain syndrome: limit depth to box squat or sub leg press. Avoid heavy loads with knee anterior to toes for extended ROM.
External rotation deficit or AC pathology: substitute safety bar or front squat to remove external-rotation demand.
FAI/labral pathology: monitor depth tolerance; box squat to limit hip flexion if symptomatic.

regression_chain.rationale: Anterior load teaches upright torso with lower load demand
Limits depth and reinforces hip-loaded pattern
Reduces shoulder demand while preserving back-loaded pattern

progression_chain.rationale: Removes stretch reflex; builds bottom-end strength
Higher upper-back/anterior-core demand at lower load
Squat pattern under dynamic load with full ROM

tactical_notes: Foundational lift for lower-body force production transferable to load carriage, casualty drag, and hill assault. Operators with sustained ruck loads should monitor lumbar tolerance and rotate to safety bar or trap bar during high-volume training blocks.

name: Front Squat

common_substitutions.context: Teaches anterior-load squat without front-rack mobility demand
Front-loads without wrist or shoulder ROM requirement
If front rack collapses repeatedly, regress to back squat while building thoracic extension
Quad-dominant alternative with reduced spinal load

common_cues: Bar sits on shoulders, not hands
Drive elbows into the sky as you stand
common_errors: Elbows drop and bar dumps forward
Excessive forward lean to chase the bar

injury_contraindications.notes: Wrist extension intolerance: use cross-grip or strap front squat to remove wrist demand.
Anterior shoulder/biceps tendinopathy: cross-grip or sub Zercher squat.
Front-loaded position is generally back-friendly but thoracic kyphotic collapse will load lumbar; emphasize upper-back position.

regression_chain.rationale: Same anterior-load pattern with simpler hand position

progression_chain.rationale: Front squat is the catch position of the clean

tactical_notes: Builds the catch position required for clean variants and develops anti-flexion trunk strength under axial load. Strong direct transfer to carrying loads at chest level (ammo cans, casualties).

name: Goblet Squat

common_substitutions.context: KB handle position is identical functionally
When DB or KB is no longer heavy enough

common_cues: Hold the bell at your chest
Elbows inside your knees at bottom
Drive elbows to ceiling, not weight forward

injury_contraindications.notes: Generally well-tolerated; if patellofemoral pain present, limit depth to pain-free range.
Lower spinal load than back squat; appropriate for most return-to-train scenarios.

progression_chain.rationale: Same pattern, higher load potential with barbell

tactical_notes: Best squat pattern for new trainees and rehab populations. Carries operator-relevant trunk-bracing demand without requiring rack equipment — useful in field gym setups.

name: Leg Press

common_substitutions.context: More natural arc than traditional leg press
Loads legs without compressive spine demand

common_substitutions.reason: rehab cleared, returning to free weights

Don't lock the knees at top
common_errors: Lumbar flexion (butt comes off pad) at deep ROM
Feet positioned too low (excess knee shear)

injury_contraindications.notes: Watch for posterior pelvic tilt at deep ROM — cut range before butt lifts. Generally safe for back-pain populations if depth is controlled.
Foot position low on platform increases anterior knee shear; place feet higher if patellofemoral pain present.
Deep flexion can provoke FAI symptoms; modify ROM.

progression_chain.rationale: Higher quad bias, similar machine support

tactical_notes: Useful return-to-train tool post lower-extremity injury when free-weight squatting is contraindicated. Limited tactical specificity due to fixed path and supine load vector.

name: Bulgarian Split Squat

common_substitutions.context: Both feet engage ground; lower stability demand

common_cues: Front foot far enough out — knee stacks over ankle
Drop the back knee straight down
Brace the trunk; no lateral lean
common_errors: Front foot too close (excess knee travel forward)
Trunk drift forward over front leg
Pushing off the back foot to stand

injury_contraindications.notes: Anterior knee pain: ensure front foot is far enough forward; reduce depth if symptomatic.
Rear hip flexor stretch can provoke flexor irritation; pad rear foot or shorten stance.

regression_chain.rationale: Bilateral foot contact with split stance

tactical_notes: Single-leg loading directly addresses asymmetry common in operator populations from boot wear, holster carry, and unilateral kit. Strong transfer to step-overs, casualty drag, and uneven-terrain locomotion under load.

name: Lunges

Drop the back knee straight down to the floor
common_errors: Step too short (knee shoots over toes)
Knee valgus on the front leg

injury_contraindications.notes: Anterior knee pain: lengthen stride and reduce depth; consider reverse lunge variant.
Hip flexor irritation: shorten ROM at rear leg.

tactical_notes: Unilateral pattern transfers to terrain navigation under load. Walking variant builds locomotion endurance applicable to ruck movement.

name: Hack Squat
common_cues: Sit deep — knees travel over toes is fine here
common_errors: Lumbar flexion at deep ROM (butt comes off pad)

injury_contraindications.notes: Significant knee travel under load — limit depth or substitute leg press if patellofemoral pain present.
Pad supports spine; modify ROM if posterior pelvic tilt occurs at depth.

tactical_notes: Supported quad-dominant work for hypertrophy blocks. Limited direct tactical transfer; useful in accumulation phases or post-injury return.

name: Zercher Squat

common_substitutions.reason: elbow pad available, prefer rack position
common_substitutions.context: Similar anterior-load demand with different hold
Anterior load without elbow crook compression

common_cues: Bar sits in elbow crook against biceps
Pad the bar — comfort allows heavier work
Elbows stay tucked tight to body
common_errors: Insufficient depth from grip discomfort cutting set short

injury_contraindications.notes: Distal biceps tendinopathy: avoid Zercher; use front rack or goblet.
Anterior load is back-friendly but requires strong upright bracing.
Removes wrist demand entirely — useful sub when wrist is the limiter on front squat.

tactical_notes: Direct transfer to picking up and carrying awkward objects (sandbags, casualties, breaching tools). Highly trunk-demanding; valuable in tactical strength blocks.

name: Overhead Squat
common_cues: Push the bar through your hands to the ceiling
Stay tall — chest up through descent
Keep the bar over the mid-foot

injury_contraindications.notes: Rotator cuff pathology or impingement: substitute front squat. Sustained overhead load with internal rotation deficit risks impingement.
Thoracic kyphosis limits ability to maintain overhead position; address mobility before loading.
Wrist extension under load can provoke symptoms; use straight-wrist neutral position.

progression_chain.rationale: Overhead squat is the catch position

tactical_notes: High-skill mobility and stability lift. Limited direct tactical demand but exposes thoracic and shoulder mobility deficits that affect rifle carry, climbing, and overhead work.

name: Pause Squat

common_substitutions.context: Sits on box at depth instead of paused
Front-load pause squat is also valid

common_cues: Stay tight in the hole — no relaxation
Same upright posture as a regular squat

injury_contraindications.notes: Sustained loaded position increases isometric spinal demand; modify or substitute box squat if symptomatic.
Sustained deep position can provoke patellofemoral symptoms.

regression_chain.rationale: Marks depth without sustained loaded pause

progression_chain.rationale: Lock in heavier loads after pause work

tactical_notes: Builds bottom-end strength and reinforces depth consistency. Useful tool in strength blocks targeting position-specific weakness.

name: Box Squat

common_substitutions.reason: shoulder ROM limit on bar position
common_substitutions.context: Same box use with safer bar position

common_cues: Sit back to the box, not down
Knees stay over feet — push hips back
Pause briefly on the box — don't bounce
Drive feet through the floor to stand
common_errors: Plopping onto the box (impact load on spine)

injury_contraindications.notes: Plopping on the box transmits compressive load through spine — controlled descent only.
Sit-back demand may provoke hip flexor crease symptoms in some lifters.

tactical_notes: Westside-style box squat builds posterior-chain force production with reduced quad demand. Useful for operators with knee pain or in deload weeks.

name: Safety Bar Squat

common_substitutions.context: Standard back squat with shoulder mobility cleared
Different bar position, similar back tax

common_cues: Drive your back into the bar
Hold the handles or use the rack
Bar tries to push you forward — fight it
common_errors: Bar shoves trunk forward (insufficient upper-back tightness)

injury_contraindications.notes: Removes external rotation demand of standard back squat — primary use case for shoulder-limited lifters.

common_errors: Hip break (turns into a regular squat)

injury_contraindications.notes: Patellar tendinopathy or post-surgical knee: extreme anterior knee shear contraindicated. Use leg extension or leg press instead.
Hyperextension pattern can stress lumbar facets.

regression_chain.rationale: Same quad isolation, less knee shear

progression_chain.rationale: Add load when bodyweight is mastered

tactical_notes: Pure aesthetic/hypertrophy work. No tactical specificity. Use sparingly given knee demand.

name: Pistol Squat

common_substitutions.context: Single-leg work without deep dorsiflexion demand

common_cues: Free leg out straight in front
Sit back and down on the working leg
Keep the working knee tracking over toes

injury_contraindications.notes: Deep single-leg flexion under bodyweight: avoid with patellofemoral pain or post-meniscal repair.
Requires ~40°+ dorsiflexion; chronic ankle restriction will compensate up the chain.
Deep flexion can provoke FAI symptoms.

regression_chain.exercise_name: Assisted Pistol with TRX or Pole

tactical_notes: Demonstrates true single-leg strength applicable to obstacle navigation, climbing, and uneven-terrain work. High mobility prerequisite limits population that can train it productively.

name: Walking Lunges

common_substitutions.context: Higher per-rep demand on working leg

common_cues: Drop the back knee toward the floor
common_errors: Step too short (knee shoots over toes)
Lateral pelvic drop on each step

injury_contraindications.notes: Anterior knee pain: lengthen stride, reduce depth, or substitute reverse lunge.
Hip flexor irritation common with high volume.

tactical_notes: Direct transfer to uneven-terrain locomotion under load. Common element of military fitness tests (USMC, Army) and tactical population conditioning protocols.

name: Reverse Lunges

common_substitutions.context: Rear foot elevated for greater demand

common_cues: Drop the back knee straight down
Drive through the front heel to return
common_errors: Pushing off the back foot to stand

injury_contraindications.notes: Generally knee-friendlier than forward lunges; useful for anterior knee pain populations.
Rear hip flexor stretch can be aggressive.

tactical_notes: Knee-friendly alternative for operators with patellofemoral issues. Maintains unilateral demand without forward knee travel.

name: Lateral Lunges

common_substitutions.context: Direct adductor work without lunge demand

common_cues: Push off the working leg to return

injury_contraindications.notes: Adductor strain: avoid until tissue tolerates the eccentric load.
Watch for valgus on the working leg.

tactical_notes: Frontal-plane work addresses adductor strength and lateral movement capacity — relevant to side-step movement under kit, breaching positions, and CIQ work.

name: Step Ups
common_cues: Drive through the heel of the working foot
Don't push off the trail foot
common_errors: Pushing off the trail leg (cheats the working leg)

injury_contraindications.notes: Generally well-tolerated; ensure box height matches mobility.

progression_chain.exercise_name: Weighted Box Step Up to Knee Drive

tactical_notes: Direct transfer to obstacle traversal, vehicle entry/exit, and stair climbing under load. Tactical-relevant pattern for ruck and kit carry populations.

name: Smith Machine Squat
common_cues: Feet slightly forward of the bar path
Rotate the bar to unrack and rerack carefully
common_errors: Feet directly under bar (anterior knee dominant)

injury_contraindications.notes: Fixed bar path can force lumbar flexion in some lifters; be wary.

name: Pendulum Squat

Same knee considerations as standard squat — modify depth if symptomatic.

name: Bench Press

common_substitutions.context: Independent arms allow individual grip rotation
Fixed path with reduced stabilization demand

common_cues: Bar to lower chest, not throat
Drive feet, push the bench away

injury_contraindications.notes: Acute rotator cuff or labral pathology: substitute floor press or machine press until cleared. Bar contact at chest places shoulder at end-range horizontal abduction with external rotation.
Distal biceps or triceps tendinopathy: monitor and adjust grip width.
Excess wrist extension under load can provoke symptoms; stack wrist over elbow.

tactical_notes: Foundational upper-body press for general strength. Indirect tactical transfer through pressing capacity for casualty extraction, breaching, and pushing maneuvers. Heavy bench prioritized in tactical strength programming for raw force output.

name: Overhead Press

Adds leg drive for higher loads

Squeeze your glutes, brace your trunk

Don't lean back to muscle through it

Maximal overhead loading with split catch

name: Deadlift

common_substitutions.context: Eliminates the lift-off shear of pulling from floor

name: Deficit Deadlift

name: Block Pull
common_cues: Bar elevated on blocks (typically 1-3 inches below knees)

tactical_notes: Useful for building lockout strength and returning to deadlifts post-injury at modified ROM.

injury_contraindications.notes: Long lever arm under load — strict spinal control required.
High eccentric demand; not appropriate within 4 weeks of strain.

tactical_notes: Hamstring-biased hinge useful for sprint-injury reduction. Operator populations with sprint demand benefit from inclusion in offseason work.

name: Single Leg RDL
common_cues: Stand on one leg, slight knee bend
Hinge at the hips, free leg drives back behind you
Free leg, torso, and head form a straight line
Square the hips — don't open them
Drive through the standing heel to return

injury_contraindications.notes: Standing-leg gluteus medius demand; modify if hip pain.
Balance demand on standing ankle.

regression_chain.exercise_name: Kickstand RDL (toe of free leg lightly touching)

tactical_notes: Critical unilateral hinge for asymmetry correction. Direct transfer to dynamic balance under load (uneven terrain, vehicle ingress/egress, recovery from imbalance under kit).

name: Dumbbell RDL

injury_contraindications.notes: Lower-load alternative to barbell RDL; safer for novice lifters.

tactical_notes: Useful entry point for new lifters learning the hinge pattern. Field-deployable when only DBs are available.

name: Good Morning
common_cues: Bar high on traps like a back squat
Same hip drive to stand as a deadlift

injury_contraindications.notes: Long lever arm with axial load creates very high lumbar shear; not appropriate for back-pain populations. Use with light loads only.
Eccentric demand on hamstrings.

regression_chain.rationale: Bar in hands instead of on back

tactical_notes: High-skill, high-risk lift. Limited tactical use given lower-shear alternatives exist (RDL, trap bar). Reserve for experienced lifters in periodized strength blocks.

name: Glute Bridge
common_cues: Lie on your back, knees bent, feet flat
Stop at hip-extension neutral — don't hyperextend
common_errors: Lumbar hyperextension instead of glute extension
Heels too far from butt (hamstring dominant)
Toe push instead of heel push

injury_contraindications.notes: Excellent rehab tool — minimal spinal load. Cue chin tuck and rib-down to avoid hyperextension.
Generally well-tolerated.

regression_chain.exercise_name: Supine Hip Lift to Wall (PT regression)

tactical_notes: Foundational glute activation drill. Useful in warm-ups, rehab, and daily mobility for desk-bound or vehicle-bound operators.

name: Cable Pull Through
common_cues: Stand facing away from cable, rope between legs
Push hips back to take the cable through your legs
Don't yank the cable with your arms

injury_contraindications.notes: Lower-load hinge appropriate for novices and rehab.

tactical_notes: Excellent hinge teach for novices learning to dissociate hip from knee. Useful in warm-ups and rehab return-to-train.

name: Hyperextension
common_cues: Hip pad just below your hip crease
Hinge at the hip — not the spine
Squeeze glutes to return to neutral
Don't hyperextend the spine at top
common_errors: Spinal extension instead of hip extension

injury_contraindications.notes: Hyperextension at top can stress lumbar facets; cue stopping at neutral.

tactical_notes: Useful posterior chain accessory and warm-up. Direct transfer to back endurance under sustained ruck load.

name: Reverse Hyperextension
common_cues: Lie face down, hips at edge of pad

injury_contraindications.notes: Often used in disc rehab settings for unloaded hip extension; control top range.

tactical_notes: Westside-popularized rehab and accessory tool. Useful for return-to-train post-lumbar injury.

name: Glute Ham Raise
common_cues: Knees on pad, feet locked under foot plate
Body straight from knees to head
Pull yourself back up using hamstrings

injury_contraindications.notes: High eccentric and concentric demand; not appropriate within 6 weeks of hamstring strain.
Knees take pressure on pad — adjust pad cushioning.

tactical_notes: Highest-output hamstring exercise per EMG studies. Strong injury-prevention value for sprint-based tactical populations.

name: Nordic Hamstring Curl
common_cues: Anchor your ankles under a sturdy pad or partner's hands
Keep body in a straight line from knees to head
Lower under hamstring control as far as possible
Catch with hands when control is lost
Push back to start using hands
common_errors: Falling to the floor without controlling descent

injury_contraindications.notes: Per Petersen et al. (BJSM 2011), reduces hamstring strain ~50% in soccer populations when programmed properly. NOT appropriate within 6 weeks of acute hamstring strain — extreme eccentric load.
Knee pad position critical to avoid bursitis irritation.
Hyperextension fault loads spine; maintain neutral position.

progression_chain.rationale: Add load via plate to chest

tactical_notes: Most evidence-supported hamstring injury reduction tool in sport science literature. Direct transfer to sprint deceleration capacity in tactical populations. Build tolerance gradually — extreme eccentric load demands progressive volume.

name: Pull Up
common_cues: Pull elbows down to your hips
common_errors: Kipping with no skill basis (shoulder strain)
Half-rep at the top (chin not over bar)
Shrug at top (uses traps over lats)

injury_contraindications.notes: Acute rotator cuff or labral pathology: substitute neutral-grip or lat pulldown until cleared.
Distal biceps tendinopathy: chin-up or neutral grip may be better tolerated.

tactical_notes: Foundational tactical pulling movement. Element of all military fitness assessments (Ranger, SF, MARSOC). Direct transfer to obstacle traversal, climbing, and casualty extraction. High-rep capacity is operational standard.

name: Chin Up

injury_contraindications.notes: Often shoulder-friendlier than overhand pull-up due to natural humeral rotation.
Distal biceps tendinopathy: monitor.

tactical_notes: Higher biceps recruitment than pull-up. Useful for tactical populations building pulling strength when overhand grip is contraindicated.

name: Barbell Row

common_substitutions.context: Each rep starts from the floor

common_cues: Hinge to about 45 degrees from vertical
Pull bar to lower chest/upper abs
Squeeze shoulder blades at the top
common_errors: Pulling to the chest (becomes upright row)

injury_contraindications.notes: Sustained hinged position under load demands neutral spine; sub chest-supported row if symptomatic.
Hinged position can provoke hip flexor symptoms.

tactical_notes: Core upper-back builder. Direct transfer to ruck-strap durability and pulling tasks. Modify if low back pain present.

name: Dumbbell Row
common_cues: Hand and knee on bench, opposite leg planted
Free hand pulls bell to lower ribs, not chest
Drive elbow up and back along your side
common_errors: Trunk rotation to lift the bell

injury_contraindications.notes: Bench support reduces spinal load — useful for back-pain populations.
Generally shoulder-friendly with proper ROM.

tactical_notes: Foundational unilateral pull addressing asymmetry. Strong direct transfer to single-arm pulling tasks (rope, casualty drag).

name: Lat Pulldown

common_substitutions.context: Heavier load possibility for strong lifters

common_cues: Sit tall, knees locked under pad
Pull the bar to your upper chest
Squeeze your lats — don't lean back excessively
common_errors: Excessive trunk lean back (becomes a row)
Pulling behind the neck (cervical strain risk)
Letting bar snap back at top

injury_contraindications.notes: Pulling behind the neck loads shoulder in maximum external rotation; pull to chest instead.
Behind-neck variant: avoid in populations with cervical pathology.

tactical_notes: Useful entry point for novices unable to perform pull-ups. Allows scalable load for hypertrophy and pull-up progression.

name: Cable Row
common_cues: Sit tall, slight forward lean OK
Drive elbows back along your sides
common_errors: Lumbar flexion at end of return

injury_contraindications.notes: Generally back-friendly with stable seated position.

tactical_notes: Useful row variant with constant tension. Adjustable handle (rope, V-grip, wide bar) varies stimulus.

name: Face Pull
common_cues: Pull rope to your forehead with elbows high
Externally rotate at the end — knuckles to the ceiling
Squeeze rear delts and rotator cuff

injury_contraindications.notes: Specifically therapeutic for shoulder health — primary use case is impingement prevention.

tactical_notes: Most-prescribed shoulder health exercise in S&C. Critical inclusion for tactical populations with chronic shoulder issues from kit carry, rifle position, and overhead work.

name: Pendlay Row
common_cues: Torso parallel to floor — not 45 degrees
Each rep starts from a dead stop on the floor
Pull bar to lower chest explosively
Reset the spine on each rep

injury_contraindications.notes: Demands strict isometric spinal control with very flexed hip; not appropriate for back-pain populations.
Sustained flexed-hip position can provoke hamstring tightness.

tactical_notes: Strict-form variant favored by powerlifters. Limited unique tactical value over standard barbell row.

name: T-Bar Row
common_cues: Straddle the bar, hinge to ~45 degrees
Lats and upper back drive the pull

injury_contraindications.notes: Hinged position demands neutral spine; modify if symptomatic.

tactical_notes: Heavy upper-back builder with V-grip bias. Useful in tactical strength blocks for ruck-relevant musculature.

name: Meadows Row
common_cues: One end of bar in landmine, opposite-side hand on knee for support
Pull bar end toward your hip
Drive elbow up and back along the side
common_errors: Trunk rotation to lift the bar

injury_contraindications.notes: Same hinged-position considerations as standing rows.
Single-arm demand allows asymmetry accommodation.

tactical_notes: Unilateral landmine variant addressing asymmetry. Limited unique tactical value but useful in hypertrophy blocks.

name: Seal Row
common_cues: Lie face down on a bench, DBs hanging
Pull bells to lower ribs along the sides
common_errors: Lifting chest off bench (uses leg drive)

injury_contraindications.notes: Excellent option for back-pain populations — fully removes spinal load.

tactical_notes: Best row variant for back-pain populations. Useful during return-to-train and high-volume hypertrophy blocks.

name: Chest Supported Row
common_cues: Chest on incline bench, feet planted
Pull DBs or barbell to lower chest

injury_contraindications.notes: Excellent for back-pain populations.

tactical_notes: Strict isolation of upper back without spinal demand. Useful during ruck-heavy training blocks to minimize cumulative back load.

name: Kroc Row
common_cues: Set up like a heavy DB row
Allow some body english on later reps

injury_contraindications.notes: High volume + intentional body english can compound spinal demand; not appropriate for back-pain populations.
Heavy grip demand; use straps.

tactical_notes: High-volume DB row for grip and back endurance. Direct transfer to high-volume pulling tasks (rope work, climbing).

name: Wide Grip Lat Pulldown
common_cues: Pull bar to upper chest, not behind neck
Drive elbows down and slightly back

injury_contraindications.notes: Wide grip is shoulder-demanding; modify grip if impingement present.
Behind-neck variant: avoid.

tactical_notes: Hypertrophy variant emphasizing lat width. Limited unique tactical value over standard pulldown.

name: Close Grip Lat Pulldown
common_cues: Drive elbows down close to body

injury_contraindications.notes: Generally shoulder-friendly with neutral or close grip.

tactical_notes: Lat-thickness emphasis. Useful in hypertrophy blocks.

name: Straight Arm Pulldown
common_cues: Stand facing cable, slight hip hinge
Pull bar from overhead to thighs in an arc

injury_contraindications.notes: Generally shoulder-friendly.

tactical_notes: Lat isolation. Useful for teaching lat engagement to novices. Limited tactical specificity.

name: Machine Row
common_cues: Adjust seat so handle aligns with chest

injury_contraindications.notes: Excellent for back-pain populations.

tactical_notes: Useful supported row for hypertrophy and rehab.

name: Inverted Row
common_cues: Bar at hip height, hang under it with body straight
Body in a straight line head to heel

injury_contraindications.notes: Generally shoulder-friendly; foundational rehab and pre-pull-up exercise.

tactical_notes: Foundational pull-up progression. Useful for tactical fitness candidates building toward bodyweight standards. Field-deployable using rifle or barbell racked on a power cage.

name: Shrugs
common_cues: Shrug shoulders straight up to your ears

injury_contraindications.notes: Avoid shoulder rolling — straight up-and-down pattern protects cervical spine.
Generally well-tolerated.

tactical_notes: Trap hypertrophy for kit-strap durability. Useful accessory for ruck-heavy populations.

name: Dumbbell Shrugs

injury_contraindications.notes: Avoid rolling pattern.

tactical_notes: Same as barbell shrug — trap hypertrophy for kit durability.

name: Rack Pull
common_cues: Bar in rack at knee height or just below
Same setup as standard deadlift from elevated start
common_errors: Using the rack pins as bounce

injury_contraindications.notes: Reduced ROM is back-friendlier than full deadlift; useful for return-to-train.

tactical_notes: Useful tool for grip strength and lockout strength. Effective when returning to deadlifts post-injury.

name: Neutral Grip Pull Up
common_cues: Pull elbows down to your hips

injury_contraindications.notes: Most shoulder-friendly pull up grip; preferred when impingement is present.
Brachialis emphasis; well-tolerated for elbow pathology.

tactical_notes: Most shoulder-friendly bodyweight pull pattern. Preferred variant for tactical populations with shoulder issues. Most rope-climbing patterns mimic this grip orientation.

name: Bicep Curl
common_cues: Squeeze biceps hard at the top

injury_contraindications.notes: Distal biceps tendinopathy: reduce volume and load.
Maintain neutral wrist.

tactical_notes: Hypertrophy isolation. Indirect tactical value through climbing pull strength and elbow flexor durability under rifle carry.

name: Tricep Pushdown
common_cues: Drive bar down to full extension

injury_contraindications.notes: Triceps tendinopathy: reduce load and lockout intensity.

tactical_notes: Isolation hypertrophy for elbow extension. Indirect tactical value through pressing lockout strength.

name: Barbell Curl
common_cues: Bar at thigh level, hands shoulder-width
common_errors: Using lower back to swing bar up

injury_contraindications.notes: Straight bar can stress wrists; sub EZ bar if symptomatic.
Tendinopathy considerations.

tactical_notes: Hypertrophy isolation. Limited direct tactical specificity.

name: Hammer Curl
common_cues: Bells at sides, palms facing each other

injury_contraindications.notes: Tendinopathy considerations.

tactical_notes: Forearm and brachialis hypertrophy. Useful for grip endurance under rifle carry.

name: Preacher Curl
common_cues: Chest on pad, arms over the front
Curl bar up — strict, no swing
Stop just before lockout to maintain tension
Don't bounce out of the bottom
common_errors: Bouncing out of bottom (biceps strain risk)
Not using full ROM at top

injury_contraindications.notes: Bottom-range stretch under load — caution with distal biceps tendinopathy.

tactical_notes: Strict bicep isolation. Limited tactical specificity.

name: Incline Dumbbell Curl
common_cues: Curl bells up without lifting elbows forward

injury_contraindications.notes: Bottom-range stretch can provoke anterior shoulder symptoms.

tactical_notes: Long-head bicep emphasis. Hypertrophy isolation.

name: Cable Curl
common_cues: Cable at low position, EZ bar or straight bar attachment
Stand with cable directly in front
common_errors: Allowing cable to pull arms forward at bottom

injury_contraindications.notes: Standard curl considerations.

tactical_notes: Hypertrophy isolation with constant tension.

name: Concentration Curl
common_cues: Sit on bench, elbow braced against inner thigh

injury_contraindications.notes: Tendinopathy considerations.

tactical_notes: Bodybuilding-style isolation. Minimal direct tactical transfer.

name: Spider Curl
common_cues: Chest on incline bench, arms hanging straight down
Curl bells up without elbow drift

injury_contraindications.notes: Tendinopathy considerations.

tactical_notes: Bodybuilding isolation. No tactical specificity.

name: EZ Bar Curl

injury_contraindications.notes: Friendlier than straight bar; primary use case for wrist-sensitive lifters.

tactical_notes: Wrist-friendlier curl variant. Useful for high-volume bicep work.

name: Skull Crusher
common_cues: Lie on bench, EZ bar overhead at arm's length
Lower bar to forehead — bend only at elbows
Drive bar back to start with triceps

injury_contraindications.notes: Triceps tendinopathy: reduce load and avoid bottom-range stretch.

tactical_notes: Triceps hypertrophy. Indirect tactical value through pressing lockout.

name: Overhead Tricep Extension
common_cues: DB or rope above head with both hands

injury_contraindications.notes: Overhead position can provoke impingement.
Long-head stretch under load; control the stretch.

tactical_notes: Long-head triceps emphasis. Hypertrophy isolation.

name: Tricep Kickback
common_cues: Bent over with one hand on bench for support
Extend at elbow until arm is straight

injury_contraindications.notes: Tendinopathy considerations.

tactical_notes: Light isolation. Limited tactical specificity.

name: Cable Overhead Tricep Extension
common_cues: Face away from cable, rope behind head

injury_contraindications.notes: Overhead position considerations.

tactical_notes: Long-head triceps emphasis with constant tension. Hypertrophy isolation.

name: Rope Pushdown
common_cues: Drive ropes apart at the bottom

injury_contraindications.notes: Tendinopathy considerations.

tactical_notes: Triceps hypertrophy isolation.

name: Wrist Curl
common_cues: Forearms on bench, hands hanging off edge, palms up
Lower DBs as far as comfortable
Curl up using only the wrists
common_errors: Using forearm flex instead of wrist

injury_contraindications.notes: Direct wrist load — modify load if pain present.

tactical_notes: Forearm hypertrophy and wrist durability. Useful for rifle and weapon grip endurance.

name: Reverse Curl

injury_contraindications.notes: Pronated curl can stress wrists; reduce load if symptomatic.
Lateral epicondylitis can be provoked.

tactical_notes: Forearm extensor hypertrophy. Useful for grip balance and elbow tendon health.

name: Lateral Raise
common_cues: Slight bend in elbows, maintain throughout
Lead with the elbows, not the hands
Raise to shoulder height — not higher
Pinkies slightly higher than thumbs at top

injury_contraindications.notes: Internal-rotation lateral raise (thumbs down) can provoke impingement; lead with elbow and keep neutral rotation.

tactical_notes: Lateral delt hypertrophy for shoulder yoke. Indirect tactical value through shoulder durability.

name: Cable Lateral Raise
common_cues: Cable at low position, opposite side hand grabs handle

injury_contraindications.notes: Same considerations as DB lateral raise.

tactical_notes: Constant-tension lateral delt isolation. Hypertrophy work.

name: Machine Lateral Raise
common_cues: Adjust seat so handles align with shoulders
Drive elbows up to shoulder height

injury_contraindications.notes: Generally shoulder-friendly with proper seat setup.

tactical_notes: Lateral delt hypertrophy isolation.

name: Front Raise

injury_contraindications.notes: Anterior delt isolation; modify if anterior shoulder pain.

tactical_notes: Anterior delt isolation. Limited unique tactical value; compound presses cover this musculature.

name: Rear Delt Fly
common_cues: Bend at hips, torso parallel to floor
Raise bells out to the sides
common_errors: Using upper back instead of rear delts
Standing too upright (turns into upright row)

injury_contraindications.notes: Hinged position; brace appropriately.
Generally shoulder-friendly.

tactical_notes: Posterior delt hypertrophy. Critical for shoulder posture in operators with chronic forward-rounded carriage from kit.

name: Reverse Pec Deck
common_cues: Chest against pad, palms facing each other
Squeeze rear delts at end ROM

injury_contraindications.notes: Generally shoulder-friendly with proper position.

tactical_notes: Supported rear delt isolation. Useful in shoulder rehab and posture work.

name: Cable Rear Delt Fly
common_cues: Drive arms out wide and back
common_errors: Using arms instead of rear delts

injury_contraindications.notes: Generally shoulder-friendly with adjustable angles.

tactical_notes: Constant-tension rear delt isolation. Useful for posture and shoulder health.

name: Upright Row
common_cues: Wide grip on bar — at least shoulder width plus
Pull bar up to mid-chest only — not chin
Stop when elbows reach shoulder height

injury_contraindications.notes: Per Andrews/Wilk shoulder pathology research, narrow-grip high upright row creates internal-rotation impingement at the painful arc. Use wide grip and limit ROM, or substitute lateral raise.

tactical_notes: Use cautiously due to impingement risk. Substitute lateral raise + face pull for the same delt + trap stimulus with lower injury risk.

name: Lu Raise
common_cues: Externally rotate the shoulder as you raise
Bells finish overhead with palms facing each other

injury_contraindications.notes: Combined external rotation and elevation; can be therapeutic but high skill demand.

tactical_notes: High-skill shoulder hypertrophy with cuff component. Bodybuilding-favored variant.

name: Leg Curl
common_cues: Lie face down, ankles under pad
common_errors: Hips lifting off pad (cheats with hip extension)

injury_contraindications.notes: Open-chain hamstring curl is well-tolerated post-strain when light loads used.
Generally knee-friendly.

tactical_notes: Hamstring isolation. Useful in volume blocks and post-strain return.

name: Leg Extension
common_cues: Adjust seat so knee aligns with machine pivot

injury_contraindications.notes: Open-chain heavy knee extension can stress patellar tendon and ACL; modify load post-ACL surgery per surgeon protocol. Useful in late-stage rehab.

tactical_notes: Quad isolation. Useful in late-stage knee rehab and hypertrophy blocks.

name: Calf Raise
common_cues: Stand with balls of feet on platform, heels free
Drive up onto balls of feet
Lower until you feel deep calf stretch
common_errors: Half-reps (no full stretch or full contraction)

injury_contraindications.notes: Direct ankle load — modify if symptomatic.
Calf strain considerations: not appropriate within 2 weeks of acute strain.

tactical_notes: Calf hypertrophy and ankle plantar flexion strength. Direct relevance to ruck distance tolerance and sprint mechanics.

name: Seated Calf Raise
common_cues: Pad over knees, balls of feet on platform

injury_contraindications.notes: Direct ankle load.

tactical_notes: Soleus emphasis. Critical for sustained ruck endurance — soleus is dominant in walking/long-distance loading.

name: Standing Calf Raise

injury_contraindications.notes: Direct ankle load.

tactical_notes: Calf hypertrophy. Useful for sprint and ruck performance.

name: Seated Leg Curl
common_cues: Adjust pad to align knees with pivot

injury_contraindications.notes: Open-chain hamstring isolation; useful in rehab.

tactical_notes: Hamstring isolation. Useful in volume blocks.

name: Hip Adductor Machine
common_cues: Drive knees together against the pads

injury_contraindications.notes: Adductor strain: avoid until tissue tolerates the eccentric load.
FAI considerations: deep hip flexion.

tactical_notes: Adductor isolation. Useful for groin injury prevention in sprint-based tactical work.

name: Hip Abductor Machine

injury_contraindications.notes: Generally well-tolerated; useful for gluteus medius strengthening post-knee or hip injury.

tactical_notes: Gluteus medius isolation. Critical for knee-tracking integrity under load — addresses common operator-population valgus tendency.

name: Donkey Calf Raise
common_cues: Bend at hips, body parallel to floor or close

injury_contraindications.notes: Direct ankle load.
Hinged position with axial load.

tactical_notes: Calf hypertrophy at different angle.

name: Tibialis Raise
common_cues: Heels against wall, toes pointed up

injury_contraindications.notes: Generally rehab-friendly; useful for shin splints prevention.

tactical_notes: Tibialis anterior strengthening. Direct shin splint prevention for high-volume running and ruck programs.

name: Plank
common_cues: Body in a straight line head to heel
Tuck the pelvis (rib down, posterior tilt)
common_errors: Focus on time over tension quality

injury_contraindications.notes: Generally rehab-friendly when cued correctly. Hip sag fault loads lumbar.
Wrist-supported variants can stress wrists; use forearm plank if symptomatic.

tactical_notes: Foundational trunk endurance test. Element of all military fitness assessments. Builds the bracing capacity required for ruck carry, weapon stability, and casualty drag.

name: Russian Twist
common_cues: Sit on floor, knees bent, lean back to ~45 degrees
Keep chest up — no spinal flexion
common_errors: Spinal flexion (loaded rotation under flexion increases disc strain)

injury_contraindications.notes: Per McGill biomechanics research, loaded rotation under spinal flexion increases disc shear and strain. Modify or substitute Pallof press / Woodchop standing variants if back pain present.

tactical_notes: Use cautiously. Better tactical-rotation alternatives exist (Woodchop, cable rotation) that don't combine spinal flexion with rotation under load.

name: Hanging Leg Raise
common_cues: Hang from bar with packed shoulders
Drive legs up to hip-flexion neutral or beyond

injury_contraindications.notes: Sustained passive hang loads rotator cuff; pack shoulders actively.
Anti-extension demand requires posterior pelvic tilt; without it, hip flexors dominate and load lumbar.

tactical_notes: Builds anti-extension trunk strength under hanging position. Direct transfer to climbing and obstacle work where hip flexion under hanging body is required.

name: Ab Rollout
common_cues: Knees on pad, ab wheel under shoulders
Roll out as far as you can without lumbar hyperextension
Pull yourself back with abs and lats

injury_contraindications.notes: End-range anti-extension under load — only progress when control is established.
Wheel forward demands shoulder strength.

tactical_notes: Highest-output anti-extension trunk exercise. Directly relevant to bracing under loaded ruck and weapon-shouldered position.

name: Cable Crunch
common_cues: Kneel facing cable, rope at neck or hands by face
Crunch downward, driving ribs toward hips

injury_contraindications.notes: Repeated loaded spinal flexion is McGill-flagged for disc populations; modify if symptomatic.
Avoid pulling rope by neck.

tactical_notes: Loaded spinal flexion. Use cautiously given availability of safer alternatives (Pallof, ab rollout, plank).

name: Pallof Press
common_cues: Stand sideways to cable, feet shoulder width
Hold handle at chest, press straight out away from body

injury_contraindications.notes: Anti-rotation work is back-friendly and rehab-appropriate.

tactical_notes: Highest-yield anti-rotation drill. Direct transfer to maintaining trunk position under asymmetric load (rifle, kit, single-side carry). McGill-recommended.

name: Dead Bug
common_cues: Lie on back, arms up, knees over hips at 90 degrees
Drive lower back into the floor
Lower opposite arm and leg slowly
Don't let lumbar arch off the floor

injury_contraindications.notes: Excellent rehab-stage trunk exercise. McGill-recommended for back-pain populations.

tactical_notes: Foundational anti-extension drill. Useful for new lifters, back-pain rehab, and warm-up activation.

name: Bird Dog
common_cues: Quadruped position, hands under shoulders, knees under hips
Extend opposite arm and leg slowly
Don't let trunk rotate or hips drop
common_errors: Hip drop on the working side

injury_contraindications.notes: McGill-recommended for back-pain populations.
Quadruped position; pad knees if symptomatic.

tactical_notes: Foundational anti-rotation drill. Used in McGill's Big 3 protocol for lumbar rehab.

name: Side Plank
common_cues: Forearm on floor, elbow under shoulder

injury_contraindications.notes: McGill-recommended for back-pain populations.
Supporting shoulder takes load; modify if symptomatic.

tactical_notes: Anti-lateral flexion strength critical for asymmetric load carriage (single-side rifle, sidearm, breach kit). McGill Big 3 component.

name: V-Up
common_cues: Drive arms and legs together to a V
Touch hands to feet at top

injury_contraindications.notes: Repeated spinal flexion under bodyweight; modify if disc-symptomatic.

tactical_notes: Common element of CrossFit and tactical conditioning. Builds anti-extension capacity.

name: Toes to Bar
common_cues: Drive toes up to the bar in one motion

injury_contraindications.notes: Sustained hang plus dynamic motion can stress cuff; pack shoulders actively. Avoid with active cuff pathology.
Hip flexor pull without anti-extension control loads lumbar.

tactical_notes: CrossFit-favored gymnastics movement. Builds anti-extension capacity under hanging position.

name: Sit Up
common_cues: Cross arms over chest or hands at temples (don't pull head)

injury_contraindications.notes: Repeated spinal flexion is McGill-flagged for disc populations; substitute Pallof or plank.
Avoid yanking on the neck.

tactical_notes: Element of military fitness tests historically. Use cautiously in high-volume contexts due to disc strain considerations.

name: GHD Sit Up
common_cues: Hips on pad, feet locked under foot plate
Lower with control to extension behind
Don't hyperextend the lumbar at bottom

injury_contraindications.notes: Repeated end-range flexion under load is high-risk per published case literature. Reports of rhabdomyolysis in CrossFit-volume contexts. Build tolerance gradually; avoid in disc populations.

tactical_notes: Use cautiously. High-volume programming has documented rhabdo risk. Build progressive tolerance over weeks; do not introduce in unconditioned populations.

name: Bicycle Crunch
common_cues: Bring opposite elbow to opposite knee

injury_contraindications.notes: Avoid neck pull.
Loaded rotation under flexion considerations.

tactical_notes: Standard high-rep ab exercise. Limited tactical specificity vs functional rotation work.

name: Woodchop
common_cues: Cable up high (or low for reverse)
Pull cable across body to opposite hip
Drive with hips and trunk, not arms

injury_contraindications.notes: Standing rotation is back-friendlier than loaded seated rotation.
Hip rotation under load.

tactical_notes: Functional rotation pattern. Direct transfer to throwing, sweeping, and asymmetric kit work.

name: Suitcase Carry
common_cues: Hold one DB or KB at side
Stand tall — don't lean toward or away from the load

injury_contraindications.notes: Excellent rehab-friendly anti-flexion drill.
Heavy unilateral load can stress shoulder; modify if symptomatic.

name: L-Sit

injury_contraindications.notes: Sustained hip flexion can provoke flexor symptoms.

Wrist extension under load at lockout.

tactical_notes: Best Oly variant for operators wanting full power expression without overhead squat mobility prerequisite. Builds rate-of-force-development. Programmed in TSAC literature for SOF populations.

name: Clean Pull

tactical_notes: Excellent strength accessory for clean — builds the pull mechanics under heavier load than competition lift allows. Useful for operators building Oly capacity without skill demand of full catch.

name: Snatch Pull

Wrist extension at lockout.

tactical_notes: Skill-development tool for snatch turnover and overhead position. Lower load than power snatch but excellent for building shoulder stability under speed. Useful warm-up movement.

name: Burpee

Repeated impact loading on wrist extension.

tactical_notes: Full-body metabolic conditioning with direct transfer to ground-fighting transitions, low-crawl-to-stand, and obstacle traversal. Programmed across military fitness tests. Form deteriorates rapidly under fatigue — quality over quantity.

Curl-finish error tears distal biceps; well-documented strongman injury.
Tire treads can lacerate hands without gloves.

tactical_notes: Classic odd-object lift. Direct transfer to vehicle extraction (lift-and-roll), debris clearance. Biceps tear risk if curled instead of pushed — reinforce push-to-fall cue. Tire is more variable than stone or sandbag.

name: Prowler Push
common_cues: Hands on high or low handles based on intent
Drive arms hard with each step
Push through to finish — don't stop

injury_contraindications.notes: Heavy loads can compromise position under fatigue.
Variable terrain on outdoor surfaces.

tactical_notes: Closed-chain power expression with no eccentric loading. Lowest injury risk of any heavy-load movement. Excellent return-to-duty conditioning. Direct transfer to vehicle push, breaching push, casualty drag.

name: Kettlebell Goblet Squat
common_cues: Hold bell by horns at chest

injury_contraindications.notes: Patellofemoral pain: limit depth.
FAI: monitor depth tolerance.
Wrist extension holding bell.

tactical_notes: Foundational squat-pattern teaching tool. Anterior load counterbalances novice lifters. Excellent warm-up movement and primary lift for new operators. Standard in StrongFirst curriculum.

name: Kettlebell Clean
common_cues: Hike the bell back like a swing
Pull bell up, then around — not over
Free hand stays slack and fast

injury_contraindications.notes: Bell impact on forearm if technique poor — sub kettlebell swing until clean is patterned.
Rack position loads wrist.
Hinge under load.

tactical_notes: Single-side power expression with lower spine demand than barbell clean. Trains rotational stability under fatigue. Excellent operator-population alternative when barbell unavailable. StrongFirst staple.

name: Kettlebell Snatch
common_cues: Pull high — bell to shoulder height
Punch through at top — bell doesn't flip over
Lock arm overhead — bicep by the ear
common_errors: Banging bell on forearm at lockout
Excessive low back hyperextension at lockout

injury_contraindications.notes: Active impingement: sub clean and press.
Banging bell on forearm if poor technique; common new-lifter complaint.
Lockout load.
Hyperextension at lockout common error.

tactical_notes: Highest-output single-implement movement. SFG (StrongFirst Girya) snatch test is benchmark tactical fitness assessment. Direct transfer to ground-to-overhead extraction tasks. Skill-limited; develop through swing and clean first.

name: Kettlebell Press
common_cues: Bell in rack — bicep by the ear
Pack the lat — pull bell down

injury_contraindications.notes: Active impingement: sub landmine press.
Hyperextension common; cue ribs down.
Wrist position in rack and at lockout.

tactical_notes: Single-side overhead expression — directly addresses asymmetric carry demands of operational kit. Trains rotator cuff and anti-rotation under load. StrongFirst signature movement; standard in tactical kettlebell programming.

name: Kettlebell Windmill
common_cues: Bell locked overhead — eyes on it
Push hip back toward the bell side
Slide free hand down inside leg
Reach for the floor — long spine

injury_contraindications.notes: Active rotator cuff or labral pathology: sustained overhead under variable load.
Loaded lateral flexion can flare disc pathology.
End-range hip hinge.
Sustained overhead lockout.

tactical_notes: Trains shoulder stability under hip mobility demand — addresses common operator deficit (locked hips, unstable shoulders). Anti-lateral-flexion under overhead load mimics tactical kit demands. Standard StrongFirst movement.

name: Foam Roll
common_cues: Not the joint — the muscle belly

injury_contraindications.notes: Avoid direct lumbar rolling — can flare facet pathology.
Avoid over varicose veins, recent surgical sites, acute injury.

tactical_notes: Pre-workout warm-up and post-workout recovery tool. Evidence for transient ROM improvement; long-term effects debated in literature. Useful for operators with high cumulative training and operational load.

name: Lacrosse Ball Release

injury_contraindications.notes: Avoid recent injury, varicose veins, surgical sites.

tactical_notes: Targeted soft-tissue work for high-tone areas. Useful for hip/glute, pec, lat, and forearm work in operators. Travels well in deployment context.

name: Couch Stretch
common_cues: Back foot up the wall — knee in corner
common_errors: Lumbar extension instead of pelvic tuck

injury_contraindications.notes: Patellar pathology: knee in deep flexion against wall — pad heavily or sub half-kneel hip flexor stretch.
Anterior hip pinch: can indicate FAI; back off if sharp.

tactical_notes: Direct targeting of rectus femoris and hip flexors — chronically tight in operators from sustained kit-loaded sitting and standing. Kelly Starrett signature movement; standard in TSAC mobility programming.

name: 90/90 Stretch

common_substitutions.context: Targets one hip at a time

common_errors: Rounded back instead of hip hinge
Pain at knee (force ER through hip not knee)

injury_contraindications.notes: Meniscus pathology: deep knee flexion in ER can provoke.
FAI: end-range hip rotation can pinch.

tactical_notes: Targets hip internal and external rotation simultaneously — addresses bilateral hip mobility deficit. Operators with sustained kit-loaded postures benefit substantially. Standard FRC (Functional Range Conditioning) movement.

name: World's Greatest Stretch
common_cues: Rotate from thoracic spine — not lumbar
common_errors: Rotation from lumbar instead of T-spine

injury_contraindications.notes: Deep lunge with rotation.
End-range hip flexion in lunge.
Overhead reach with rotation.

tactical_notes: Multi-joint mobility movement — hits hip flexors, hip ER, T-spine extension, and overhead in one flow. Standard dynamic warmup. Time-efficient for operators with limited mobility windows.

name: Cat Cow
common_cues: Hands under shoulders — knees under hips

injury_contraindications.notes: Sustained wrist extension; sub on forearms if symptomatic.
Excessive neck extension on cow phase.

tactical_notes: Foundational spinal articulation. Useful warm-up movement and morning mobility tool. Direct relevance for operators decompressing after heavy kit loading.

name: Band Pull Apart
common_cues: Pull band apart — chest stays still

injury_contraindications.notes: Generally well-tolerated; avoid heavy band with active impingement.

tactical_notes: High-rep posterior shoulder activation. Excellent warm-up before pressing. Standard prehab for operators with heavy bench and overhead pressing volume. Counters pec dominance.

name: Band Dislocate
common_cues: Don't force — back off if pinch

injury_contraindications.notes: Active impingement: stay in pain-free range.

tactical_notes: Foundational shoulder mobility for overhead lifts. Essential prerequisite for snatch and overhead squat. Daily mobility tool for operators with heavy pressing programming.

name: Pigeon Stretch
common_cues: Front shin perpendicular if mobile / parallel if not
Walk hands forward — fold over front leg
common_errors: Knee pain (force through hip not knee)
Forward lean before settling into hip

injury_contraindications.notes: Meniscus pathology: front knee in flexion + abduction can provoke. Sub supine figure 4.
FAI: end-range external rotation.

tactical_notes: Targets glutes, piriformis, and external rotators. Direct relevance for operators with chronic glute and posterior hip tightness from sustained sitting and load-bearing.

name: Child's Pose
common_errors: Insufficient hip ROM (heels far from glutes)

injury_contraindications.notes: Meniscus pathology: deep knee flexion.
Plantarflexion under bodyweight.

tactical_notes: Restorative mobility position. Useful for operators decompressing after high-volume training. Standard yoga and mobility programming foundation.

name: Scorpion Stretch
common_cues: Lie face down — arms in T position
Drive top leg over and across
Keep both shoulders pinned to floor

injury_contraindications.notes: Acute disc pathology with rotation: sub supine spinal twist.
Sustained T-position can flare anterior shoulder.
End-range hip rotation.

tactical_notes: Combines T-spine rotation, hip mobility, and anterior shoulder stretch. Useful pre-workout for athletes training rotational sports. Limited tactical specificity.

name: Hip Circle
common_cues: Band above knees — slight knee bend
Push knees out — feel the glutes fire

injury_contraindications.notes: Pre-existing patellofemoral issues: monitor.

tactical_notes: Glute medius activation and hip ER warm-up. Primary use is pre-squat warm-up to fire dormant glute medius. Direct relevance for operators with chronic glute amnesia from sustained sitting.

name: Banded Good Morning
common_cues: Band over upper back — feet wide

injury_contraindications.notes: Hinge under load even with band; maintain neutral spine.
Active strain: limit ROM.

tactical_notes: Excellent hinge-pattern teaching tool and warm-up for heavier hinge work. Band provides feedback without spinal compression. Standard in TSAC warmup programming.

name: Kettlebell Halo
common_cues: Hold bell by horns — close to face
Circle bell around head — close to skull
common_errors: Lumbar hyperextension at back of circle

injury_contraindications.notes: Active impingement: sub band dislocate.
Bell impact risk if technique poor — close to head not face.

tactical_notes: Shoulder mobility plus stability under variable load. Excellent warm-up before pressing. Standard StrongFirst movement. Useful for operators with chronic shoulder tightness from kit loading.
